title: The Outdated Message
special_element: The letter is dated yesterday — sealed yesterday, describing a town that has not stood in fifty years, written as though the writer walked its streets this morning.
summary: A letter arrives — the ink still wet at the crease, the wax seal unbroken, the paper smelling of pine resin and cold road.
body: A letter arrives — the ink still wet at the crease, the wax seal unbroken, the paper smelling of pine resin and cold road. The handwriting inside describes Callow's Ford in fond, exact detail: the mill with the red waterwheel, the widow Sarn who keeps the only inn, the garrison bell that rings the hour. Callow's Ford burned to the ground fifty years ago. No one has lived there since.
Special element: The letter is dated yesterday — sealed yesterday, describing a town that has not stood in fifty years, written as though the writer walked its streets this morning.
next_steps: **Stakes:** Someone — or something — is either trapped in a moment fifty years gone or has found a way to walk back into it. Either way, the road to Callow's Ford leads somewhere the maps no longer show.
**Next steps:** Follow the road to where the town stood. Find ash, foundations, and the red waterwheel still turning over rubble. Find no widow Sarn. Find the bell ringing the hour.

**Twists:** The letter was addressed to a party member by name, and the seal bears a mark — a family sigil, a guild stamp — the recipient knows but cannot explain. The widow Sarn is still there, alone, and does not know fifty years have passed.

title: Ropeless Anchorage
special_element: Every crew that anchors in the bay loses its rope by morning — not cut, not rotted, but consumed thread by thread, as if something under the water is being paid in fiber. The *Sable Debt*'s captain has done the arithmetic no one else bothered with: the more rope she brings, the calmer the crossing. She wants to know what she is feeding — and whether she can feed it more.
summary: Ropeless Anchorage Category: Story Hook · Tier: 3 · Biome: coast · Mood: unsettling
body: Ropeless Anchorage Category: Story Hook · Tier: 3 · Biome: coast · Mood: unsettling

They racked the anchor rope new last night — fresh hemp, stiff and yellow, stinking of tar. By dawn it lies in a grey heap on the deck of the *Sable Debt*, unwound to a dust so fine the wind lifts it one strand at a time. And still the ship holds at anchor in Black Lantern Bay, riding steady, moored to nothing at all.

Special element: Every crew that anchors in the bay loses its rope by morning — not cut, not rotted, but consumed thread by thread, as if something under the water is being paid in fiber. The *Sable Debt*'s captain has done the arithmetic no one else bothered with: the more rope she brings, the calmer the crossing. She wants to know what she is feeding — and whether she can feed it more.
next_steps: **Stakes:** in a fortnight the *Sable Debt* is due to carry a noble's household across open water — no bay to soften the crossing, and nothing left to anchor with if the rope habit fails her.

**Next steps:** the captain hires the party to stand a night-watch over a test coil and see what actually takes it, or to dive the anchorage floor before dawn, while whatever fed there has gone quiet.
**Twists:** the rope is not destroyed. It is being spent — reappearing rewoven as the very lines other ships swear they never brought aboard.

title: The Pardon That Wasn't
special_element: Every forgery Vance Quill sells carries the same seal — he traced it off an old document without knowing what it was. It isn't a magistrate's mark. It's the oath-sigil of the Coin-Hand, the guild that owns every loan, marker, and broken knee in this district, and to them a pardon stamped with it doesn't read *cleared*. It reads *vouched in*. The party didn't slip the law. They've been signed into a debt they never took out, and the first of three payments comes due whether they understand the arrangement or not.
summary: Category: Story Hook · Tier: 2 · Biome: urban · Mood: grim
body: Category: Story Hook · Tier: 2 · Biome: urban · Mood: grim

A runner catches you outside the tavern, chest heaving, and presses a folded chit into your palm before you can wave him off. It's stamped in the same red wax as the pardon you bought, and under the seal a clerk's hand has written: *DEBT SETTLED, FIRST OF THREE.* He won't say who sent him. He won't meet your eyes.

Special element: Every forgery Vance Quill sells carries the same seal — he traced it off an old document without knowing what it was. It isn't a magistrate's mark. It's the oath-sigil of the Coin-Hand, the guild that owns every loan, marker, and broken knee in this district, and to them a pardon stamped with it doesn't read *cleared*. It reads *vouched in*. The party didn't slip the law. They've been signed into a debt they never took out, and the first of three payments comes due whether they understand the arrangement or not.
next_steps: Stakes: refuse the debt and the Coin-Hand collects the old way — property, favors, or fingers. Pay it and the party stands recognized as members, owing two more installments.
Next steps: follow the runner to a counting-house where a ledger keeper is already inking the party's names into the guild rolls — in ink, not pencil.
Twists: Quill has no idea his seal means anything. Corner him and his confusion is real, which makes the Coin-Hand's belief in the pardon scarier, not less.

title: The Salt Archive's Debt
special_element: The debt is already part-paid. Sometime in the last year, one PC lost a person from memory — a face, a name, the weight of a voice — and never felt the gap, because the archive took that memory as a down payment before the page was ever found. Settling what's owed means first uncovering what was already taken, and from whom.
summary: Category: Story Hook · Region: none · Tier: 3 · Biome: ruins · Mood: melancholy
body: Category: Story Hook · Region: none · Tier: 3 · Biome: ruins · Mood: melancholy

A page in the Library of Salt bears one of your names, in a hand none of you recognise. Touch it and fine white grain sifts free onto your fingers — dry, cold, faintly bitter on the air — and the page is thinner than it was a breath ago.

Special element: The debt is already part-paid. Sometime in the last year, one PC lost a person from memory — a face, a name, the weight of a voice — and never felt the gap, because the archive took that memory as a down payment before the page was ever found. Settling what's owed means first uncovering what was already taken, and from whom.
next_steps: Stakes: every day the debt goes unanswered, the named page thins further; when the last grain sifts away, so does whatever the archive is still owed in full — a life, a promise, or the final trace of that missing memory.
Next steps: the ledger names a creditor by title, not name — "the one who lent the winter." Tracking down who or what that is becomes the party's real quest.

Twists: the debt was never the party's to begin with. It was inherited, sold, or lost in a wager by someone they trusted — and the archive doesn't care whose signature is on it, only whose name is on the page.

title: Cold Light Rising
special_element: The cold light doesn't spread like weather — it spreads like an invitation, one harbour a night, always to the town nearest the last one taken. Something has to say yes first. Every stricken harbourmaster's logbook ends the same way: a bargain struck at low tide, a price the town could not refuse, and a signature. The party isn't racing a plague up the coast. They're racing to reach the next harbour before its harbourmaster signs.
summary: Cold Light Rising Category: Story Hook · Tier: 4 · Biome: coast · Mood: grim · Difficulty: hard
body: Cold Light Rising Category: Story Hook · Tier: 4 · Biome: coast · Mood: grim · Difficulty: hard

Three nights ago the harbour at Sallow Reach went dark, then wrong. Every lantern on every mast now burns a cold blue that throws no heat and no shadow, and this morning the whole fishing fleet sat riding at anchor with not one rope over the side. The tide brought back no wreckage and no bodies. It brought a letter, addressed to whoever is in charge, in the harbourmaster's own hand: *"We let it in. You will too."*

Special element: The cold light doesn't spread like weather — it spreads like an invitation, one harbour a night, always to the town nearest the last one taken. Something has to say yes first. Every stricken harbourmaster's logbook ends the same way: a bargain struck at low tide, a price the town could not refuse, and a signature. The party isn't racing a plague up the coast. They're racing to reach the next harbour before its harbourmaster signs.
next_steps: Stakes: at one harbour a night, the port the party actually cares about — home, patron, base of operations — is four nights from the light reaching its door.
Next steps: reach the next threatened harbour ahead of the tide; find out what is being offered, and to whom, before the ink dries.

Twists: the bargain isn't coercion. Every harbourmaster who signed was drowning — in debt, in plague, in ruin — and the cold light *fixed it*. What's on offer is a living town, in exchange for its harbour becoming the next Black Lantern Bay.
